abstract.t: This document defines JSON Abstract Data Notation (JADN), an information modeling language designed to improve the rigor of Internet technical specifications. Existing standards like ABNF, XSD, and JSON Schema define formal syntax for text and structured data values. But while RFC 3444 and RFC 8477 establish goals for information modeling, adoption remains limited. JADN addresses this by defining a regular information type structure that balances simplicity with extensibility and expressive power. The key feature of any IM is equivalence across data formats, and unlike traditional schema languages, a JADN schema is itself an information value. This ensures equivalence across schema representations, allowing authors to select or design a representation suitable for the application and target audience.

section.name: Introduction
section.t: begins: "There has been ongoing confusion about the differences between Information Models and Data Models for defining managed objects in network management." The confusion can be partially attributed to the absence of a definition of information itself, so this specification begins with two:

dl.dd: structured, processed data that has been given context, meaning, and semantics within a specific domain. It turns raw facts into usable knowledge by organizing data objects, their attributes, and their relationships, typically at a high level of abstraction, independent of physical storage protocols.
the reduction of uncertainty, measured as the statistical rarity of a message rather than its semantic meaning. It quantifies how much a data point reduces doubt, measured in bits.

section.t: Given a literal value, the information in that literal can be viewed as the questions it answers. An information model (IM) defines equivalence between literals, and a concise literal such as an RFC 791 IP packet contains the identical information (both semantics: "What is the source address?" and entropy: "32 bits") as an equivalent verbose literal in JSON or XML format.
An implementable IM specification must also define Information Value, Data Value, and the relationships between them:

dl.dd: the value of a variable (a typed unit of state) in a processing environment.

a fixed sequence of bytes usable outside a processing environment. Also known as a Document, Message, File, Stream, Data Value, or Lexical Value. Because literals are immutable they can be compared for equality and hashed or signed for integrity.

definition of the content of an information or data value. An IM data type has three properties: an information value space, a literal space for each data format, and a small set of functions and relations including ordering of the value space and mappings from each literal space to the value space. This definition extends the XML Schema Datatype to information modeling by including literal spaces and mappings for multiple data formats. Both information and data values are instances of Type.

the implementation of a variable in a processing environment. An IM object has a type, a current value, and the behavior defined for variables of that type. Objects are instances of Class. Note that this is not specific to object-oriented programming languages: K&R C has an "int" type and the definition of int variables (the "class") has behavior such as addition and comparison in addition to the value restrictions defined by the type. Also note that the Javascript language defines Boolean, String, Number, Array, Map and Set objects in chapters 20-24. JSON confusingly uses "object" to refer only to the literal values of Map objects, not any others. This specification defines object to include all types as in Javascript, not just Map objects as in JSON.

a named equivalence mapping between information values and literals. More than one data format can be defined for a single media type, for example Record information values can be represented as either Map or Array literals in the same media type depending on data format.

section.t: For an IM to be useful it must be abstract and language-independent. An abstract schema is a high level specification that separates information sharing requirements from implementation details. This is desirable for initial conceptual design where details are unknown or undecided, and for exposition and publication where they are distracting. But robust implementation requires unambiguous specification of schema-independent encoding rules to translate between information values and messages. An IM defines both information values and message representations in a way that does not depend on programming language or data format.
An IM can be used to support system development in three ways:

ol.li: Pseudocode: describes message content without mechanically generating or processing anything
Template: generates one or more concrete schemas (data models) more consistently than writing DMs directly, while reusing existing message processing software

IM: validates and processes message content directly, enabling lossless translation among data formats. This specification defines the required observable result of lossless round-trip translation, not necessarily an optimal algorithm for achieving that result.

section.name: Examples
section.t: The remainder of this specification defines the structure of a JADN schema as a set of fixed-layout type definitions along with namespaced package metadata common to all types. The following example representations of a simple GPS Coordinate type definition illustrate that a JADN schema is an information value, that the information value is identical regardless of data format, and what that value looks like when represented in equivalent data formats. The formats shown are:

ul.li: JSON data: the normative storage format for JADN schemas
XML data: an XML format composed of JADN elements, called XML Abstract Schema Definition (XASD)
JIDL text: JADN Information Definition Language, an example non-normative domain-specific language.
Property Tables: a popular data standards publication format, shown as Markdown source. Tables in HTML or XML markup formats are also possible.

figure.name: JADN XASD Format
figure.artwork: <?xml version="1.0" ?> <Schema version="2.0"> <Types> <Type name="Coordinate" type="Record">GPS Coordinate <Field fid="1" fname="latitude" type="Number" minInclusive="-90.0" maxInclusive="90.0">Latitude</Field> <Field fid="2" fname="longitude" type="Number" minExclusive="-180.0" maxInclusive="180.0">Longitude</Field> </Type> </Types> </Schema>

figure.name: JADN IDL Format
figure.artwork: Coordinate = Record // GPS Coordinate 1 latitude Number=[-90.0, 90.0] // Latitude 2 longitude Number=(-180.0, 180.0] // Longitude

figure.name: JADN Property Table, Markdown Format
figure.artwork:
**Type: Coordinate (Record):** GPS Coordinate
| ID | Name | Type | \# | Description | |----|---------------|------------------------|----|-------------| | 1 | **latitude** | Number=[-90.0, 90.0] | 1 | Latitude | | 2 | **longitude** | Number=(-180.0, 180.0] | 1 | Longitude |

section.name: Requirements Language
section.t: The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.

section.name: Body
section.t: Body text

section.name: Simple Unordered (Bullet) List
section.t: Text before the list
Text after the list.

section.name: Ordered List With Lowercase Letters in Brackets Instead of Numbers
Continuous Numbering in a List That is Split by Text or Sections
section.t: Text to introduce the list
Some text in between

section.name: Section in-between
section.t: More text in-between

section.name: Lists With Hanging Labels
section.t: the list item is indented the value of indent

dl.dd: With a label shorter than the indent.
With a label longer than the indent.

section.name: Source Code Examples
section.t: This is an example C program

section.name: Using xref
section.t: A reference to
A reference to

section.name: IANA Considerations
section.t: This memo includes no request to IANA.

section.name: Security Considerations
section.t: This document should not affect the security of the Internet.

front.title: XML Schema Definition Language (XSD) 1.1 Part 2: Datatypes
Key words for use in RFCs to Indicate Requirement Levels

abstract.t: In many standards track documents several words are used to signify the requirements in the specification. These words are often capitalized. This document defines these words as they should be interpreted in IETF documents. This document specifies an Internet Best Current Practices for the Internet Community, and requests discussion and suggestions for improvements.

section.name: Appendix 1
section.t: This becomes an Appendix

section.name: Contributors
section.t: Thanks to all the contributors.
